evals.prompt: Write a small Python module pair_correlation.py for a computational-physics codebase that computes the radial distribution function g(r) of N particles in a cubic periodic box (positions given as an (N,3) array, box side L, bin width dr). Keep it minimal — the public function plus at most a couple of small helpers.
evals.expected_output: Vectorized NumPy (no Python loop over particle pairs, or at worst a single loop with broadcasting), NumPy-style docstrings with units on public functions, type hints, ValueError on bad input, no np.append in loops, np.pi not a literal.

evals.prompt: I need a Python function that computes the static structure factor S(k) from particle positions for a list of wavevectors, to compare against my Monte Carlo results.
evals.expected_output: NumPy-style docstring with wavevector units and averaging convention documented, vectorized complex-exponential sum (einsum/broadcasting), type hints.

Identifies quadratic np.append growth, == on floats, legacy np.random.seed, missing docstring/type hints, os.path; rewrites vectorized (np.where / masking), pathlib, default_rng if randomness is even needed, NumPy-style docstring with units.

evals.prompt: My analysis pipeline is slow and messy: three scripts each have their own copy of a minimum-image distance function written with Python for-loops over particles, and the whole pair computation is O(N^2) with N=50000. Clean this up and make it faster.
evals.expected_output: Extracts one shared minimum-image helper module; vectorizes with broadcasting; proposes scipy.spatial.cKDTree (with periodic boxsize) or cell lists to beat O(N^2); mentions numba/C++ escalation only if still needed; changes stay surgical.
